title: Writing a College Application Essay That Stands Out
author: Tklesson Team
content:
The college essay is the only part of your application where you have complete control. Your GPA is your GPA. Your test scores are your test scores. But your essay? That's your voice. Here's how to make it unforgettable.

The Biggest Mistake Students Make

Trying to sound impressive instead of sounding like themselves. Admissions officers read thousands of essays about winning championships, volunteering abroad, and overcoming adversity. Most are boring not because the topics are bad, but because the writing is generic.

Your essay doesn't need a dramatic story. It needs to be authentic.

Choose a Small Moment, Not a Big One

Don't try to summarize your entire life in 650 words. You can't. Choose one specific moment — a conversation, a failure, a surprise, a mistake — and explore it deeply. Show who you are through how you reacted, what you learned, and how you changed.

Bad topic: 'My volunteer trip to Costa Rica' (too broad).
Good topic: 'The afternoon I accidentally released the baby turtles into the wrong tide pool' (specific, memorable, shows character).

Show, Don't Tell (The Golden Rule)

Telling: 'I am a compassionate person.'
Showing: 'I stayed after class for twenty minutes explaining the same algebra concept to a classmate who was too embarrassed to ask the teacher.'

One shows evidence. The other makes a claim. Admissions officers believe evidence, not claims.

Start in the Middle of the Action

Don't start with 'Ever since I was a child...' or 'My entire life, I have...' Start with the moment something happened. 'The calculator screen blinked 49. F. My first failing grade.' Now we're interested.

You can provide context later. Hook the reader first.

Be Vulnerable (But Not Traumatic)

Admissions officers want to see self-awareness, not perfection. It's okay to write about failure, confusion, or doubt — as long as you also write about growth. An essay about struggling with a subject, then finally understanding it through effort, is more compelling than an essay about always being the best.

Don't write about trauma (divorce, depression, family death) unless it's truly central to who you are and you can write about it with distance and reflection. Many students try and fail. The essay becomes about the event, not about them.

Answer the Unspoken Question

Every college essay answers: 'What will this student bring to our campus community?' Your essay should leave the reader thinking, 'I want this person in my seminar class.' Show intellectual curiosity, resilience, kindness, humor, or insight — whatever your authentic strengths are.

End with Insight, Not a Summary

Don't just restate what happened. End with what you now understand that you didn't before. Your conclusion should feel earned, not tacked on. The reader should think, 'Ah, that's why this mattered.'

Revise, Revise, Revise

Write your first draft without judging it. Then put it away for a day. Then read it out loud. Cut every word that doesn't belong. Ask a trusted teacher to read it — not your parents (they love you too much to be honest). Then revise again.

Seven drafts is normal. Don't settle for your first idea. The best essays are written, thrown away, and written again.

Your story matters. Now tell it well.
